title: Kubernetes Deployments Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Pod self-healing, ReplicaSets, and zero-downtime updates.

questions.question: Why is it considered a bad practice to create standalone Pods directly in Kubernetes?

options.text: Because they cannot be deleted once created.
Because they cannot have labels attached to them.
Because if the Pod or its Node crashes, Kubernetes will not automatically restart or replace it.
Because they can only run a single container.

questions.question: Which Kubernetes object sits directly beneath a Deployment and actively ensures that the specified number of Pod replicas are running at all times?

options.text: LoadBalancer
Ingress
ReplicaSet
VolumeClaim

questions.question: How does a Kubernetes Deployment achieve 'Zero-Downtime' when updating a container image from v1 to v2?

options.text: By executing a 'Rolling Update', replacing old pods with new ones one at a time, ensuring health checks pass before terminating the old ones.
By instantly killing all v1 pods and instantly starting all v2 pods simultaneously.
By redirecting DNS queries to an entirely new cluster.
By merging the v1 and v2 codebases automatically.

questions.question: If a newly deployed image (v2) causes your API pods to crash continuously, which command instantly reverts the infrastructure back to the stable v1?

options.text: kubectl delete pods --all
kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name>
kubectl apply -f v1.yaml --force
kubectl reset cluster

questions.question: In a Deployment YAML file, what is the 'selector.matchLabels' field used for?

options.text: To tell the ReplicaSet which specific Pods it is responsible for managing based on their labels.
To select which physical servers the pods should run on.
To map incoming HTTP requests to specific URLs.
To link the Deployment to a specific GitHub repository.
